text: Robotic assisted coiled sleeve gastrectomy to ruin wide gastric bypass.
The patient is a forty-six year old female with history of severe gastroesophageal reflux and severe dysphagia after a laparoscopic sleeve gastrectomy performed in two thousand eighteen.
The workup included an upper GI swallow that showed a kinked sleeve in two areas and an EGD that confirmed the findings.
The surgeon begins the operation by dissecting the sleeve gastrectomy, dividing adhesions between the omentum and the suture line.
The surgeon carefully dissects the hiatus and passes a Penrose drain around the retroesophageal window.
The surgeon performs a detailed dissection of the hiatus, carefully separating the surrounding tissues.
The surgeon identifies a previous hernia repair with VLOG suture during the dissection process.
The surgeon dissects the esophagus into the mediastinum, achieving a three-centimeter intra-abdominal portion.
The surgeon closes the hiatus using two O-etibon sutures, securing the area previously dissected and prepared.
An intraoperative esophagogastroduodenoscopy (EGD) is performed to visually inspect the proximal sleeve for any kinks or structural abnormalities.
The surgeon transects the sleeve approximately five centimeters from the gastroesophageal junction and proceeds to resect the antrum, removing a portion of the stomach.
The surgeon performs a jejuno-jejunostomy, connecting two segments of the jejunum using a technique similar to a ring or classic bypass.
The surgeon performs a two-layer gastrojejunostomy, creating an eighty-centimeter alimentary limb and a fifty-centimeter biliopancreatic limb.
Following the completion of the Roux-en-Y gastric bypass, an intraoperative EGD is performed to visually confirm the integrity of the gastrointestinal tract.
The operative time was two hundred and fifty minutes and the blood loss was minimal.
The patient was discharged on day number one after an upper GI swallow that showed good passage without evidence of a leak and tolerating a blenderized diet.
